body_txt: A (usually) unspecified date somewhere between the end of World War I in 1918 and the commencement of World War II in 1939. A time of ladies in evening gowns and gentlemen in dinner jackets mingling at well-to-do cocktail parties; rich tweed-clad country men surveying their lands or hunting game; and hard-boiled detectives who are veterans of World War I. Lots of action takes place in big country houses and small surrounding villages, often involving, depending on the author/genre, either wacky romantic misunderstandings or cold-blooded acts of murder, both of which revolve around complex, labyrinthian schemes. In more urban areas (usually either London or New York City ), there's lots of Art Deco , swanky parties, heavy drinking, and gay repartee. While The Roaring '20s and The Great Depression both took place during this period, the conservative and patrician milieu of the Genteel Interbellum Setting tends to keep the era's upheavals somewhat at arm's length (unless they are deliberately included to show how real-world social, cultural, and political changes ultimately affect everyone in some way). The conservative Nostalgia Filter towards the era might have something to do with the fact that, in hindsight, it was the twilight of Britain's time as a leading world power; the aftermath of World War II and the Cold War would see the United Kingdom relegated to second tier status behind the United States and the Soviet Union. This trope was formerly named "Christie Time", after the period when most (if not all) of Agatha Christie 's Hercule Poirot novels are thought to be set (they actually cover a time period of 1916 to the early 1970s, suggesting that Poirot lives to be over a hundred years old ), and when all screen adaptations of her work are set. It could well have been called Wodehouse Time also. The historical name for this period is the Interbellum, hence the trope name. Later portrayals may see it combined with Diesel Punk . In his short story Umney's Last Case , Stephen King refers to a temporal variant, Chandler American Time . Here, the action is set at the very end of the period, just before America enters the World War II in 1941. In both Genteel Interbellum Setting and Chandler American Time , the time from 1918-1941 is usually idealized , while in Diesel Punk it is the opposite, often containing critical deconstruction of the values of those times. See also Old, Dark House , which is usually the setting for Ten Little Murder Victims . Compare and contrast The Gay '90s , Big Fancy House , Victorian Novel Disease .
